abstract: Gradient normalization is central in deep-learning optimization because it stabilizes training and reduces sensitivity to scale. For deep architectures, parameters are naturally grouped into matrices or blocks, so spectral normalizations are often more faithful than coordinatewise Euclidean ones; Muon is the main motivating example of this paper. More broadly, we study a family of spectral normalization rules, ranging from ordinary gradient descent to Muon and intermediate Schatten-type schemes, in a mean-field regime where parameters are modeled by probability measures. We introduce a family of Spectral Wasserstein distances indexed by a norm gamma on positive semidefinite matrices. The trace norm recovers the classical quadratic Wasserstein distance, the operator norm recovers the Muon geometry, and intermediate Schatten norms interpolate between them. We develop the static Kantorovich formulation, prove comparison bounds with W2, derive a max-min representation, and obtain a conditional Brenier theorem. For Gaussian marginals, the problem reduces to a constrained optimization on covariance matrices, extending the Bures formula and yielding a closed form for commuting covariances in the Schatten family. For monotone norms, including all Schatten cases, we prove the equivalence between the static and dynamic Benamou-Brenier formulations, deduce that the resulting transport cost is a genuine metric equivalent to W2 in fixed dimension, and show that the induced Gaussian covariance cost is also a metric. We then interpret the associated normalized continuity equation as a Spectral Wasserstein gradient flow, identify its exact finite-particle counterpart as a normalized matrix flow, obtain first geodesic-convexity results, and show how positively homogeneous mean-field models induce a spectral unbalanced transport on the sphere.

citations.fullCitation: [14] Keller Jordan, Yuchen Jin, Vlado Boza, Jiacheng You, Franz Cesista, Laker Newhouse, and Jeremy Bernstein. Muon: An optimizer for hidden layers in neural networks. Blog post, 2024.
citations.justification: This work introduces the Muon optimizer, which serves as the primary motivating application for the main paper. The paper's central goal is to provide a theoretical foundation for Muon and similar spectral normalization methods by framing them as gradient flows within a novel 'Spectral Wasserstein' geometry.
citations.title: Muon: An optimizer for hidden layers in neural networks

citations.fullCitation: [9] Lénaïc Chizat and Francis Bach. On the global convergence of gradient descent for over-parameterized models using optimal transport. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, volume 31, pages 3040–3050, 2018.
citations.justification: This paper is a key predecessor that established the connection between the mean-field limit of training neural networks and gradient flows in the classical Wasserstein space. The current paper builds directly upon this paradigm by replacing the standard Wasserstein geometry with its new family of Spectral Wasserstein geometries to account for matrix-based spectral normalizations.
citations.title: On the global convergence of gradient descent for over-parameterized models using optimal transport

citations.fullCitation: [4] Jean-David Benamou and Yann Brenier. A computational fluid mechanics solution to the Monge–Kantorovich mass transfer problem. Numerische Mathematik, 84(3):375–393, 2000.
citations.justification: This is the foundational paper for the dynamic (fluid mechanical) formulation of optimal transport. The main paper heavily relies on and extends this framework in Section 3 to prove that its static Spectral Wasserstein cost is equivalent to a dynamic formulation, which is crucial for establishing that it is a genuine metric and for characterizing its geodesics.
citations.title: A computational fluid mechanics solution to the Monge–Kantorovich mass transfer problem

citations.fullCitation: [19] François-Pierre Paty and Marco Cuturi. Subspace robust wasserstein distances. In Proceedings of the 36th International Conference on Machine Learning, volume 97 of Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, pages 5072–5081, 2019.
citations.justification: The main paper explicitly identifies this work as the 'closest antecedent' to its approach. Paty and Cuturi's work robustifies the Wasserstein distance by maximizing the transport cost over a family of projected quadratic costs, which is conceptually very similar to the max-min representation of the Spectral Wasserstein distance derived in Theorem 2.10.
citations.title: Subspace robust wasserstein distances

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Muon Dynamics as a Spectral Wasserstein Flow

**1. Authors and Institution(s)**

The author of this research paper is Gabriel Peyré, affiliated with CNRS and ENS, PSL Université. The paper was released on arXiv in April 2026.

**2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape**

This work is positioned within the field of deep learning optimization, specifically focusing on the role of gradient normalization techniques for matrix-shaped parameters, such as weight blocks in neural networks. Gradient normalization is a widely adopted practice in modern deep learning due to its capacity to stabilize training and mitigate sensitivity to scale in high-dimensional parameter spaces. Muon is highlighted as a contemporary and influential example of a spectral normalization procedure that serves as a primary motivating application for this research.

The paper adopts a mean-field perspective, a growing trend in the analysis of wide neural networks, where the collective behavior of an arbitrarily large number of parameters is described using probability measures. This approach builds upon foundational works in landscape analysis (Mei et al. [16]), optimal transport convergence analysis (Chizat and Bach [9]), and metric-gradient-flow viewpoints (Ambrosio et al. [1]). This work differentiates itself by moving beyond the traditional Euclidean Wasserstein geometry to introduce and utilize matrix-aware Spectral Wasserstein geometries.

In the domain of optimal transport, this research is related to generalized coupling costs and weak transport theory (Gozlan et al. [13], Backhoff-Veraguas et al. [3, 2]), covariance-dependent transport costs (Burger et al. [6]), the dynamic optimal transport framework of Benamou and Brenier [4], and matrix-valued optimal transport (Ning et al. [18], Chen et al. [8]). A distinguishing feature of this work is its focus on transporting scalar probability measures while penalizing a global matrix norm of the displacement covariance.

The paper also connects to research on robust Wasserstein distances, particularly those involving optimization over transport costs. It draws parallels with subspace robust Wasserstein distances (Paty and Cuturi [19]), which generalize max-sliced Wasserstein by optimizing over higher-dimensional subspaces. The proposed Spectral Wasserstein distance is presented as a generalized positive semidefinite (PSD)-norm version of this "max-over-cost" concept. This perspective is related to metric learning in quadratic settings (Flamary et al. [12], Cuturi and Avis [11]) and transportation models with congestion (Carlier et al. [7]). The paper explicitly contrasts its approach—robustification through concave maximization over admissible quadratic costs, leading to a globally convex static problem—with methods that minimize transport objectives with respect to cost, which often result in non-convex problems (Sebbouh et al. [21]).

Finally, the work contributes to the literature on normalized and spectrally normalized first-order optimization methods. It contextualizes its idealized Muon dynamics within the broader framework of norm-constrained linear minimization oracles (Pethick et al. [20]) and studies on how normalized gradient methods alter optimization dynamics (Cutkosky and Mehta [10], Murray et al. [17]). This paper's analysis of Muon is presented as an idealized limit, stripping away stochasticity, continuous-time dynamics, and momentum components, to rigorously identify and study its underlying mean-field Spectral Wasserstein geometry.

**3. Key objectives and motivation**

The central motivation of this research is to establish a rigorous mathematical framework that explains and generalizes modern deep-learning optimization techniques, particularly spectral normalization procedures like the Muon optimizer. Gradient normalization is a standard practice, and for deep architectures, where parameters are often grouped into matrices or blocks, spectral normalizations are frequently employed for their ability to respect the matrix geometry of the parameter space. Muon, as an iconic example, exemplifies these benefits by stabilizing training and reducing sensitivity to scale.

The primary objective is to describe a comprehensive family of spectral normalization procedures—ranging from ordinary gradient descent to Muon and various intermediate Schatten-type rules—within a mean-field regime. This regime is characterized by an arbitrarily large number of parameters, naturally modeled as a probability measure over the space of neurons.

To achieve this, the paper aims to:
* **Introduce Spectral Wasserstein distances:** These distances are defined by a norm $\gamma$ on positive semidefinite matrices. The choice of $\gamma$ allows for a continuum of geometries: the trace norm recovers the classical quadratic Wasserstein distance (corresponding to ordinary gradient descent), the operator norm recovers the Muon geometry, and intermediate Schatten norms provide interpolations between these extremes.
* **Develop the static Kantorovich formulation:** This involves defining the Spectral Wasserstein cost based on an infimum over couplings, where the cost is determined by the $\gamma$-norm of the displacement covariance matrix.
* **Establish theoretical properties of the static cost:** This includes proving comparison estimates with the standard $W_2$ distance, deriving a max-min representation using convex duality (Theorem 2.10), and obtaining a conditional Brenier theorem that specifies conditions under which optimal couplings are induced by transport maps (Corollary 2.11).
* **Analyze Gaussian marginals:** For Gaussian probability distributions, the goal is to show how the infinite-dimensional transport problem can be reduced to a finite-dimensional optimization over covariance matrices, defining a generalized covariance cost that extends the classical Bures formula (Theorem 2.12). A closed-form solution for commuting covariances within the Schatten family is also sought (Corollary 2.13).
* **Connect static and dynamic formulations:** For a specific class of "monotone norms" (which includes all Schatten norms), a key objective is to prove the equivalence between the static Kantorovich formulation and the dynamic Benamou-Brenier formulation (Theorem 3.3). This equivalence is crucial for establishing that the Spectral Wasserstein distance is a genuine metric and for identifying its geodesics (Corollary 3.4, 3.5).
* **Interpret normalized dynamics as gradient flows:** The paper aims to interpret the associated normalized continuity equation as a Spectral Wasserstein gradient flow, identifying its exact finite-particle counterpart as a normalized matrix flow (Corollary 4.5). This provides a continuum perspective on discrete optimization algorithms.
* **Investigate geodesic convexity:** The study of geodesic convexity for functionals over these new geometries is an objective, aiming to characterize linear functionals (Theorem 5.2) and the relative entropy (Theorems 5.4, 5.5).
* **Apply to neural network models:** The research seeks to demonstrate how these geometries apply to mean-field models of two-layer Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs) and, for positively two-homogeneous models, how the problem can be reduced to a spectral unbalanced transport on the sphere (Proposition 6.3, 6.4).
* **Provide numerical demonstrations:** To illustrate the theoretical concepts, the paper includes numerical experiments comparing static couplings and MMD gradient flows for different Schatten norms (trace, Frobenius, operator norms).

In essence, the overarching motivation is to move beyond heuristic understandings of matrix normalization in deep learning by grounding these techniques in a rigorous, geometric framework derived from optimal transport theory. This provides a unified language for analyzing and comparing diverse normalization schemes and for interpreting their effects on the dynamics of parameter updates.

**4. Methodology and approach**

The methodology employed in this paper is primarily theoretical, constructing a novel mathematical framework for Spectral Wasserstein distances and their associated gradient flows, complemented by numerical illustrations.

**4.1. Formalizing Spectral Wasserstein Geometry:**
The core of the approach involves introducing a family of "Spectral Wasserstein distances" indexed by a norm $\gamma$ on the cone of $d \times d$ positive semidefinite matrices ($S^d_+$).
*   **Parameterization by Matrix Norms:** The paper defines these distances using $\gamma$, with a particular focus on the Schatten $p$-norms, $\gamma_p(S) = \|S\|_{S_p}$. These norms offer a continuous interpolation: $p=1$ (trace norm) corresponds to the classical quadratic Wasserstein distance, $p=\infty$ (operator norm) corresponds to the Muon geometry, and $p=2$ (Frobenius norm) represents an intermediate case.
*   **Duality and Representing Sets:** The concept of a convex compact representing set $K_\gamma \subset S^d$ is introduced, such that $\gamma(S) = \max_{Q \in K_\gamma} \text{tr}(QS)$ for $S \succeq 0$. For "monotone norms" (those for which $0 \preceq S \preceq T \implies \gamma(S) \leq \gamma(T)$), $K_\gamma$ can be chosen within $S^d_+$.

**4.2. Static Kantorovich Formulation:**
*   **Generalized Static Cost (Definition 2.3):** The $W_\gamma(\mu, \nu)^2$ is defined as the infimum of $\gamma(\text{Cov}(\text{displacement}))$ over all couplings $\pi \in \Pi(\mu, \nu)$, where $\text{Cov}(\text{displacement})$ is the covariance of the difference between coupled points.
*   **Comparison and Max-Min Representation:** The paper proves that $W_\gamma$ is topologically equivalent to the classical $W_2$ distance (Proposition 2.8). A key methodological step is the derivation of a cost-robust max-min representation (Theorem 2.10): $W_\gamma(\mu, \nu)^2 = \max_{Q \in K_\gamma} W_Q(\mu, \nu)^2$, which transforms the problem into finding the optimal quadratic transport cost from a set of admissible costs. This duality is established using Sion's theorem.
*   **Conditional Brenier Theorem:** Using the max-min formulation, the paper demonstrates that if the maximizing matrix $Q^*$ is positive definite and the source measure $\mu$ is absolutely continuous, an optimal coupling is induced by a Monge map (Corollary 2.11), similar to classical optimal transport theory.
*   **Gaussian Reduction:** For Gaussian marginals, the problem is explicitly reduced to a finite-dimensional optimization over cross-covariance matrices, defining a generalized Bures distance (Theorem 2.12). A closed-form solution is derived for commuting covariances under Schatten norms (Corollary 2.13).

**4.3. Dynamic Benamou-Brenier Formulation and Metric Properties:**
*   **Monotonicity Requirement:** For the dynamic formulation, the additional assumption that $\gamma$ is a monotone norm is crucial, ensuring $K_\gamma \subset S^d_+$.
*   **Dynamic Cost (Definition 3.1):** The $W_{BB}^\gamma(\mu_0, \mu_1)^2$ is defined as the infimum of the integral of the $\gamma$-norm of the velocity covariance along a path $(\mu_t, v_t)$ satisfying the continuity equation.
*   **Static-Dynamic Equivalence:** The paper's structural core is proving the equivalence $W_{BB}^\gamma(\mu_0, \mu_1) = W_\gamma(\mu_0, \mu_1)$ (Theorem 3.3). This equivalence establishes $W_\gamma$ as a bona fide metric on $P_2(\mathbb{R}^d)$ and allows for the identification of constant-speed geodesics (Corollary 3.4, 3.5).

**4.4. Spectral Wasserstein Gradient Flows:**
*   **Duality Map:** A local tangent norm $N_\mu(v)^2 = \gamma(\int v(x)v(x)^T d\mu(x))$ is introduced. The "duality map" $J_\mu(g)$ is defined as the velocity field minimizing the linearized decrease of a functional $f$ penalized by this tangent norm (Definition 4.1).
*   **Structure and Finite-Particle Interpretation:** A structure theorem identifies $J_\mu(g)(x) = -(Q^*_\mu)^{-1}g(x)$ where $Q^*_\mu$ is an active matrix in $K_\gamma$ (Theorem 4.2). This leads to a normalized continuity equation. For empirical measures, this continuum flow translates directly to finite-dimensional normalized particle dynamics, $\dot{X}_t = \Xi_p(\nabla F_n(X_t))$, with explicit matrix selectors $\Xi_p$ for Schatten norms (Proposition 4.4, Corollary 4.5).
*   **Gaussian Preservation:** Conditions are derived for spectral flows to preserve Gaussian measures, simplifying the PDE evolution to ODEs on means and covariances (Corollary 4.6).

**4.5. Geodesic Convexity Analysis:**
*   The paper investigates $\kappa$-geodesic convexity (Definition 5.1).
*   **Linear Functionals:** It provides exact criteria for geodesic convexity of linear functionals $F_h(\mu) = \int h(x)d\mu(x)$, linking it to the convexity of $h$ and a matrix inequality involving $\nabla^2 h(z)$ and $Q \in K_\gamma$ (Theorem 5.2).
*   **Relative Entropy:** The geodesic convexity of relative entropy is established under conditions of uniform ellipticity of $K_\gamma$ (Theorem 5.4) and, by approximation, a weaker statement is derived for the more general singular case where $K_\gamma$ may contain singular matrices (Theorem 5.5).

**4.6. Application to MLPs and Unbalanced Transport:**
*   The framework is connected to mean-field models of two-layer MLPs.
*   For positively two-homogeneous models, a projection method is used to reduce the ambient transport problem to a "spectral unbalanced transport" geometry on the sphere $S^{d-1}$ (Proposition 6.2, 6.3, 6.4). This identifies a new class of unbalanced transport problems beyond the classical Wasserstein-Fisher-Rao action.

**4.7. Numerical Experiments:**
*   **Static Couplings:** The discrete Spectral Wasserstein problem is formulated as a convex optimization problem, solved using CVXPY for $p=1, 2, \infty$.
*   **MMD Gradient Flows:** MMD objective with an energy-distance kernel is minimized using explicit Euler updates based on the derived $\Xi_p$ for $p=1, 2, \infty$.
These experiments visually demonstrate how different Schatten norms influence coupling structures and particle trajectories.

**5. Main findings and results**

This research introduces a novel framework for analyzing matrix-normalized optimization procedures in deep learning through the lens of optimal transport theory.

*   **Spectral Wasserstein Distance as a Unified Geometry:** The paper defines a family of "Spectral Wasserstein distances," $W_\gamma$, parameterized by a norm $\gamma$ on positive semidefinite matrices. This framework provides a unified geometric interpretation for various gradient normalization schemes:
    *   The trace norm ($\gamma_1$) recovers the classical quadratic Wasserstein ($W_2$) geometry, corresponding to ordinary gradient descent.
    *   The operator norm ($\gamma_\infty$) recovers the Muon geometry, thereby providing a mathematical foundation for this specific deep learning optimizer.
    *   Intermediate Schatten $p$-norms ($\gamma_p$, $1 < p < \infty$) offer a continuous interpolation between these extremes.

*   **Static Properties of $W_\gamma$:**
    *   **Comparison with $W_2$:** The Spectral Wasserstein distance $W_\gamma$ is bounded above and below by multiples of $W_2$, implying that it induces the same topology on the space of probability measures (Proposition 2.8).
    *   **Cost-Robust Duality:** $W_\gamma$ is equivalent to a max-min problem, $W_\gamma(\mu, \nu)^2 = \max_{Q \in K_\gamma} W_Q(\mu, \nu)^2$, where $K_\gamma$ is the dual unit ball of $\gamma$. This formulation describes $W_\gamma$ as a robust Wasserstein distance that optimizes over admissible quadratic costs (Theorem 2.10).
    *   **Conditional Monge Map:** Under conditions where a maximizing matrix $Q^*$ is positive definite and the source measure is absolutely continuous, optimal couplings for $W_\gamma$ are shown to be induced by Monge maps (Corollary 2.11).
    *   **Gaussian Reduction:** For Gaussian marginals, the infinite-dimensional problem reduces to a finite-dimensional optimization over cross-covariance matrices, defining a generalized Bures distance, $B_\gamma(\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1)$. For commuting covariances in the Schatten family, a closed-form solution for this distance is provided (Theorem 2.12, Corollary 2.13).

*   **Dynamic Properties and Metricity (for Monotone Norms):**
    *   **Static-Dynamic Equivalence:** For the class of monotone norms (which includes all Schatten norms), the paper proves that the static Kantorovich formulation $W_\gamma$ is equivalent to a dynamic Benamou-Brenier formulation $W_{BB}^\gamma$ (Theorem 3.3).
    *   **Genuine Metric:** This equivalence establishes $W_\gamma$ as a bona fide metric on the space of probability measures $P_2(\mathbb{R}^d)$ (Corollary 3.4).
    *   **Geodesics:** Geodesics in this geometry are explicitly identified as constant-speed displacement interpolations between measures, based on optimal static couplings (Corollary 3.5).

*   **Spectral Wasserstein Gradient Flows:**
    *   **Normalized Transport PDE:** The paper interprets the associated normalized continuity equation as a Spectral Wasserstein gradient flow. The velocity field for this flow is given by a "duality map" $J_\mu(g)(x) = -(Q^*_\mu)^{-1}g(x)$, where $g$ is the functional gradient and $Q^*_\mu$ is an active matrix from the dual set $K_\gamma$ (Theorem 4.2).
    *   **Finite-Particle Counterpart:** For empirical measures, this continuum flow corresponds to an explicit finite-dimensional normalized particle dynamics, $\dot{X}_t = \Xi_p(\nabla F_n(X_t))$, with specific matrix selectors $\Xi_p$ for Schatten norms. This explicitly connects standard gradient descent ($p=1$) and Muon ($p=\infty$) to their geometric counterparts (Proposition 4.4, Corollary 4.5).
    *   **Gaussian Preservation:** Conditions are derived under which the spectral flow preserves Gaussian measures, allowing for a reduction to finite-dimensional ODEs on means and covariances (Corollary 4.6).

*   **Geodesic Convexity:**
    *   **Linear Functionals:** Linear functionals $F_h(\mu) = \int h(x)d\mu(x)$ are geodesically convex if and only if $h$ is convex. For $C^2$ functions $h$, $\kappa$-geodesic convexity is equivalent to $\nabla^2 h(z) \succeq \kappa Q$ for all $Q \in K_\gamma$ (Theorem 5.2). For Schatten norms, this simplifies to $\nabla^2 h(z) \succeq \kappa \text{Id}$.
    *   **Relative Entropy:** Relative entropy is shown to be $\kappa$-geodesically convex under conditions where $K_\gamma$ contains only positive definite matrices and a curvature condition on the potential $V$ holds (Theorem 5.4). A weaker, approximate convexity result is provided for cases where $K_\gamma$ includes singular matrices, relevant for Schatten norms with $p > 1$ (Theorem 5.5).

*   **Application to MLPs and Unbalanced Transport:**
    *   The framework is relevant for mean-field models of two-layer MLPs.
    *   For positively two-homogeneous models (common in neural networks), the ambient Spectral Wasserstein flow induces a spectral unbalanced transport geometry on the sphere. The classical Wasserstein-Fisher-Rao reduction is recovered only in the trace-norm case, indicating a new class of spherical transport problems for other norms (Proposition 6.3, 6.4, Remark 6.5).

*   **Numerical Validation:** Numerical experiments demonstrate the distinct characteristics of static couplings and MMD gradient flows for the trace, Frobenius, and operator norms. The operator-norm flow exhibits more globally coordinated particle movements, while the trace-norm flow is more local, with the Frobenius norm interpolating between these behaviors (Section 7).

**6. Significance and potential impact**

This research contributes to the fundamental understanding of optimization algorithms in deep learning by providing a rigorous mathematical and geometric framework. Its significance and potential impact span several areas:

*   **Foundational Understanding of Deep Learning Optimizers:** The paper offers a continuum interpretation of matrix-normalized optimizers like Muon, translating empirical observations into a rigorous geometric language rooted in optimal transport theory. This provides a formal basis for understanding why these optimizers are effective, particularly for large-scale, deep architectures.
*   **Unified Perspective on Normalization:** By introducing the Spectral Wasserstein distance, the work unifies diverse gradient normalization techniques—from classical gradient descent to modern spectral methods—under a single mathematical umbrella. This enables a systematic analysis and comparison of their underlying geometric implications, rather than treating them as isolated heuristics.
*   **Novel Class of Metrics:** The Spectral Wasserstein distances themselves represent a new class of metrics on probability measures. They extend the well-established optimal transport framework to scenarios where collective interactions and matrix-valued costs are central, opening new avenues for research in metric geometry and its applications.
*   **Advanced Analytical Tools:** The development of tools such as the static-dynamic equivalence, max-min representations, generalized Bures distances, and geodesic convexity criteria provides researchers with a robust toolkit to analyze the properties (e.g., convergence, stability, landscape characteristics) of optimization dynamics induced by these new geometries.
*   **Insights into Optimization Dynamics:** The findings clarify how different matrix-aware normalizations alter the effective "metric" of the parameter space, consequently changing the steepest-descent paths. Numerical results visually demonstrate that operator-norm flows, akin to Muon, induce more globally coordinated movements, while trace-norm flows are more local, offering insights into the behavioral differences of these algorithms.
*   **Bridge Between Disciplines:** The paper effectively bridges the fields of optimal transport, metric geometry, and machine learning optimization. This interdisciplinary approach can foster further cross-pollination of ideas, leading to innovations in both theoretical understanding and algorithmic development.
*   **Guidance for Algorithm Design:** A deeper theoretical understanding of these geometries could inform the design of new, more principled, and potentially more performant matrix-normalized optimization algorithms. Instead of relying solely on empirical tuning, future optimizers might be designed with specific geometric properties in mind.
*   **Future Research Directions:** The paper identifies several open problems, such as a sharper characterization of optimal couplings, extending global convergence theories (e.g., the Chizat-Bach theory) beyond the classical $W_2$ setting, and incorporating genuinely blockwise normalization schemes used in practical optimizers. The spherical reduction for homogeneous models also introduces a new class of unbalanced transport geometries, presenting further research opportunities.
overview:
Modern deep learning relies heavily on gradient normalization to stabilize the training of large-scale models. When parameters are grouped into matrices—such as the weights of a linear layer or the attention blocks in a transformer—these normalization schemes often take a "spectral" form, accounting for the underlying matrix geometry. A prominent example is the Muon optimizer, which uses a specific matrix-normalized gradient update to achieve faster convergence and better scaling properties compared to standard stochastic gradient descent.

While Muon and similar methods are empirically successful, their theoretical foundations have remained relatively unexplored. This research addresses this gap by introducing a mathematical framework that interprets these optimization dynamics as gradient flows in a new family of geometries known as Spectral Wasserstein distances. By viewing neural network parameters through a "mean-field" lens—where an arbitrarily large number of neurons are represented by a probability distribution—the study provides a unified geometric language for understanding how different normalization choices reshape the path to convergence.

![MMD trajectories](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04891v1/mmd_trajectories_three.png)
*Figure 1: Comparison of particle trajectories for MMD gradient flows using trace ($p=1$), Frobenius ($p=2$), and operator ($p=\infty$) Schatten norms. The operator norm flow, corresponding to Muon, exhibits globally coordinated particle movements.*

## The Spectral Wasserstein Distance

The classical quadratic Wasserstein distance, denoted as $W_2$, is a cornerstone of optimal transport. It measures the minimum effort required to transform one probability distribution into another by considering the sum of squared distances between individual points. Mathematically, it is defined as:

$$W_2(\mu, \nu)^2 = \inf_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu, \nu)} \int \|x-y\|^2 d\pi(x, y)$$

where $\mu$ and $\nu$ are probability measures, and $\pi$ is a "coupling" that describes how mass is moved from $x$ to $y$. 

The core contribution of this work is to generalize this concept by replacing the Euclidean distance with a cost function based on matrix norms. Specifically, the paper introduces the Spectral Wasserstein distance $W_\gamma$, defined as:

$$W_\gamma(\mu, \nu)^2 = \inf_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu, \nu)} \gamma\left( \int (x-y)(x-y)^T d\pi(x, y) \right)$$

In this definition, $\gamma$ is a norm acting on $d \times d$ positive semidefinite matrices. Instead of summing the squared distances, we compute the covariance matrix of the displacements $(x-y)$ and then measure the "size" of that covariance matrix using $\gamma$.

## Schatten Norms and Interpolation

The choice of the norm $\gamma$ allows the framework to cover a continuous range of geometries. The paper focuses on Schatten $p$-norms, denoted as $\gamma_p$, which act on the singular values of a matrix. Three cases are particularly important:

1.  **Trace Norm ($p=1$):** When $\gamma$ is the trace norm, $W_\gamma$ exactly recovers the classical $W_2$ distance. This is because the trace of the displacement covariance matrix is exactly the integral of $\|x-y\|^2$. This geometry corresponds to standard gradient descent.
2.  **Operator Norm ($p=\infty$):** This norm measures only the largest singular value of the covariance matrix. This choice corresponds to the Muon geometry, where gradients are normalized such that only the most significant directions of change are penalized.
3.  **Frobenius Norm ($p=2$):** This provides an interpolation between the local, point-wise focus of $W_2$ and the global, spectral focus of Muon.

By varying $p$, researchers can smoothly transition between standard optimization and the more coordinated, spectral updates used in modern practice.

## Robust Transport and Admissible Costs

A powerful way to understand $W_\gamma$ is through its "dual" representation. The paper proves that $W_\gamma$ can be viewed as a "robust" or "adversarial" version of optimal transport. For any norm $\gamma$, there exists a set of admissible cost matrices $K_\gamma$. The distance can be rewritten as:

$$W_\gamma(\mu, \nu)^2 = \max_{Q \in K_\gamma} W_Q(\mu, \nu)^2$$

In this formulation, $W_Q$ is a Mahalanobis-type Wasserstein distance where the cost of moving from $x$ to $y$ is weighted by a matrix $Q$, defined as $(x-y)^T Q (x-y)$. The Spectral Wasserstein distance effectively searches for the "worst-case" matrix $Q$ in the set $K_\gamma$ that maximizes the transportation cost.

This perspective reveals that Muon ($p=\infty$) is equivalent to finding the single direction in space along which the two distributions are hardest to align. Conversely, $W_2$ ($p=1$) assumes that all directions contribute equally to the total cost.

![Static matchings](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04891v1/static_matchings_three.png)
*Figure 2: Static optimal matchings between two point clouds for different Spectral Wasserstein geometries. As $p$ increases, the matching becomes less sensitive to individual outliers and more focused on the global alignment of the distributions.*

## From Static Metrics to Optimization Dynamics

While the static distance helps characterize the geometry, optimization is a dynamic process. The paper establishes a bridge between the static cost and the continuous evolution of parameters by proving an equivalence with the Benamou-Brenier formulation.

For certain norms (known as monotone norms), the distance between two measures is equal to the minimum "energy" required to flow from one to the other along a path $\mu_t$ with velocity $v_t$:

$$W_{BB}^\gamma(\mu_0, \mu_1)^2 = \inf_{(\mu_t, v_t)} \int_0^1 \gamma\left( \int v_t(x)v_t(x)^T d\mu_t(x) \right) dt$$

This energy integral is subject to the continuity equation, $\partial_t \mu_t + \nabla \cdot (\mu_t v_t) = 0$, which ensures mass is conserved. This result is significant because it allows us to define "Spectral Wasserstein Gradient Flows." These are the steepest-descent paths a distribution would take to minimize a functional (like a loss function) under the $W_\gamma$ metric.

## Finite-Particle Gradient Flows and Muon

In practice, we don't optimize continuous distributions; we optimize $n$ discrete particles (or neurons). The paper shows that the continuum spectral flow translates directly into a concrete update rule for a parameter matrix $X_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}$.

If we want to minimize a loss function $F_n(X_t)$, the "spectral" update rule is:

$$\dot{X}_t = - \Xi_p(\nabla F_n(X_t))$$

where $\Xi_p$ is a normalization operator that depends on the Schatten norm $p$. For a gradient matrix $G$, let its singular value decomposition be $G = U \text{diag}(\sigma) V^T$. The normalization is given by:

$$\Xi_p(G) = \frac{U \text{diag}(\sigma^{q-1}) V^T}{\|\sigma\|_q^{q-2}}$$

where $q$ is the conjugate exponent such that $1/p + 1/q = 1$.

-   **When $p=1$ (Standard GD):** The operator $\Xi_1$ is just a constant scaling, and we recover standard gradient descent.
-   **When $p=\infty$ (Muon):** The operator $\Xi_\infty$ performs a "whitening" operation, $G (G^T G)^{-1/2}$, effectively making all non-zero singular values equal to 1. This is exactly the core update used in the Muon optimizer.
-   **When $p=2$ (Frobenius):** The operator $\Xi_2$ simply divides the gradient by its Frobenius norm, $G / \|G\|_F$, which is a common form of global gradient clipping.

## Geodesic Convexity and Global Convergence

A major question in optimization is whether a local descent method will find a global minimum. In optimal transport, this is often linked to "geodesic convexity"—the idea that the loss function looks "bowl-shaped" along the shortest paths (geodesics) in the chosen geometry.

The paper analyzes when a functional $F(\mu)$ is convex relative to $W_\gamma$. For linear functionals of the form $F_h(\mu) = \int h(x) d\mu(x)$, the study finds that they are convex if and only if the underlying function $h$ is convex. More specifically, for a smooth function $h$, the condition for "curvature" $\kappa$ is:

$$\nabla^2 h(z) \succeq \kappa Q \text{ for all } Q \in K_\gamma$$

For the Muon geometry ($p=\infty$), this requirement is quite stringent: it implies that $h$ must be convex in every possible direction simultaneously. This provides a theoretical lens to evaluate how normalization affects the "flatness" or "sharpness" of the optimization landscape.

## Significance for Neural Networks

The researchers apply this framework to mean-field models of two-layer Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs). In these models, neurons are often assumed to be "homogeneous"—meaning if you scale the weights, the output scales predictably.

The paper demonstrates that for such models, the Spectral Wasserstein flow in the high-dimensional weight space can be reduced to a specific type of "unbalanced transport" on the sphere $S^{d-1}$. This finding is significant because it suggests that Muon and its relatives are not just normalizing the size of the updates, but are fundamentally changing how the *directions* of the neurons evolve on the unit sphere. 

While classical $W_2$ flows on the sphere lead to the well-known Wasserstein-Fisher-Rao geometry, spectral flows lead to a new, broader class of spherical geometries that prioritize global alignment over individual neuron movement.

## Conclusion

By grounding spectral normalization in the theory of optimal transport, this work moves beyond heuristic explanations for optimizers like Muon. It establishes that these methods are not merely "tricks" to speed up training, but are the natural gradient flows associated with a rigorous family of matrix-aware metrics.

The Spectral Wasserstein framework provides a unified map of the optimization landscape, showing how different norms—from trace to operator—allow practitioners to choose between local, Euclidean updates and global, spectral updates. As neural networks continue to grow in complexity, these geometric insights offer a principled way to design and analyze the next generation of deep learning optimizers.

keyInsights: Spectral Wasserstein distances offer a robust Wasserstein distance framework, equivalent to optimizing over admissible quadratic transport costs, allowing for a precise geometric interpretation of matrix normalizations.
The equivalence between the static and dynamic formulations establishes Spectral Wasserstein as a genuine metric, providing a foundation for analyzing optimization landscapes and geodesic paths.

Different Schatten norms (e.g., trace, Frobenius, operator) induce distinct gradient flow behaviors, where operator-norm flows (Muon) lead to more globally coordinated particle movements compared to the local movements of trace-norm flows.
originalProblem: A lack of rigorous mathematical and geometric understanding for widely used spectral normalization procedures, such as the Muon optimizer, in deep learning.
The need for a unified framework to analyze and generalize various gradient normalization techniques, especially for matrix-shaped parameters in deep neural networks.
Developing a mean-field perspective that can describe the collective behavior of an arbitrarily large number of parameters in a geometrically consistent way.
results: The Spectral Wasserstein distance $W_\gamma$ is topologically equivalent to the classical $W_2$ distance and can be represented as a max-min problem $\max_{Q \in K_\gamma} W_Q(\mu, \nu)^2$.

For Gaussian marginals, the problem reduces to a finite-dimensional optimization over cross-covariance matrices, defining a generalized Bures distance, with a closed-form solution for commuting covariances under Schatten norms.

The continuum Spectral Wasserstein gradient flow corresponds to an explicit finite-dimensional normalized particle dynamics, $\dot{X}_t = \Xi_p(\nabla F_n(X_t))$, with specific matrix selectors $\Xi_p$ for Schatten norms, connecting directly to standard gradient descent (p=1) and Muon (p=$\infty$).
solution: Introducing Spectral Wasserstein distances, $W_\gamma$, parameterized by a norm $\gamma$ on positive semidefinite matrices, to unify various normalization schemes (trace norm for classical $W_2$, operator norm for Muon).
Developing both static (Kantorovich) and dynamic (Benamou-Brenier) formulations for $W_\gamma$, and proving their equivalence for monotone norms.

Interpreting normalized dynamics as Spectral Wasserstein gradient flows and identifying their exact finite-particle counterparts, thus connecting continuous-time theory to discrete optimization algorithms.
summary.summary: This research develops a mathematical framework for matrix-normalized deep learning optimizers by introducing Spectral Wasserstein distances, which provide a unified geometric interpretation for gradient descent, Muon, and intermediate Schatten-type normalizations. The work establishes these distances as bona fide metrics and identifies their associated gradient flows, rigorously connecting continuous-time dynamics to discrete optimization algorithms.

title: Muon Dynamics as a Spectral Wasserstein Flow
